perturbation.original_text: Independent Contractor will provide the Company with prompt prior written notice of such request or requirement so that the Company may seek an appropriate protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: Independent Contractor will provide the Company with notice of such request or requirement within 3 days of receiving it, regardless of whether the Company is able to seek an appropriate protective order.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the notice period to a strict 3-day limit may not allow the company sufficient time to seek a protective order, conflicting with legal standards regarding reasonable opportunity to respond to legal demands.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45(d)(2)(B) requires that a party objecting to a subpoena must do so before the earlier of the time specified for compliance or 14 days after service. The contract language of 3 days limits the time to take appropriate action.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text ensures the company receives "prompt prior written notice" to seek a protective order. The changed text imposes a strict 3-day notice, regardless of the company's ability to seek a protective order. FRCP 45(d)(2)(B), as discussed in the Law Explanation, requires a party objecting to a subpoena to do so within 14 days. While not explicitly present in the provided snippet, the Law Explanation's reference to this rule, combined with the Perturbation Explanation highlighting the insufficient time for response, suggests a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Rule 45, addresses subpoenas and emphasizes compliance requirements. The 3-day limit in the changed text potentially undermines the company's ability to comply with subpoena response requirements, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of the Agreement, Independent Contractor shall redeliver all tangible Confidential Information furnished by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of the Agreement, Independent Contractor shall redeliver all tangible Confidential Information furnished by the Company within 60 days.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of confidential information to 60 days after termination may violate the duty to protect trade secrets, which typically requires immediate return.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. A 60 day delay in returning confidential information upon termination could be considered unreasonable and fail to adequately protect trade secrets against misappropriation, especially given the immediate impact termination can have.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 60-day delay in returning confidential information, which contradicts the requirement to protect trade secrets. The DTSA, as suggested by the Law Explanation, requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. A 60-day delay could be considered unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions civil actions related to trade secret misappropriation, indicating the importance of protecting trade secrets and enforcing related obligations, which the delay potentially undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Except to the extent Independent Contractor is advised in writing by counsel that such action is prohibited by law, Independent Contractor will also destroy all written material, memoranda, notes, and other writings or recordings whatsoever prepared by it based upon, containing, or otherwise reflecting any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Independent Contractor will retain all written material, memoranda, notes, and other writings or recordings whatsoever prepared by it based upon, containing, or otherwise reflecting any Confidential Information for a period of 1 year.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring the independent contractor to retain confidential information for one year after termination directly contradicts typical expectations of immediate destruction or return, potentially violating trade secret protection obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines misappropriation to include use of a trade secret. Retaining confidential information for a year after termination, even without actively using it, increases the risk of use and could be seen as violating reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets. States that adopted versions of the UTSA expect quick action.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation and law explanation both highlight how retaining confidential information post-termination increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation, contravening the spirit and intent of the UTSA, which emphasizes protective measures. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the change from destroying confidential information to retaining it for a year directly opposes the reasonable efforts expected to safeguard trade secrets, fitting under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction. This creates an unenforceable or illegal term because retaining increases risk of violating state and federal trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: Independent Contractor acknowledges and agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Exhibit B by Independent Contractor and that the Company shall be entitled to specific performance, including without limitation injunctive relief, as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Independent Contractor acknowledges and agrees that money damages would be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Exhibit B by Independent Contractor. The Company is not entitled to specific performance or injunctive relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that money damages are sufficient and denying the possibility of injunctive relief limits the company's remedies in a way that may conflict with the available remedies under trade secret law, which often includes injunctive relief to prevent further misuse of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings to prevent actual or threatened misappropriation
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 allows for injunctive relief in cases of misappropriation of trade secrets. The modification denies access to this remedy, contradicting federal law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the Company is not entitled to specific performance or injunctive relief, which directly contradicts the remedy of injunctive relief available under 18 U.S. Code § 1836, as stated in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 2, which mentions that the Attorney General may obtain injunctive relief. The original text allows for injunctive relief, while the changed text denies it, creating a clear contradiction with the law.
